exchanges.speaker: coach
exchanges.text:
Notes session — P6 (world_005_marrick, field register)

**Observation 1 — the tail is a loop-guard violation the guard never got a chance to fire on.** The verdict is explicitly licensed at Turn 26: *"The verdict is licensed now — both hands meet in one name."* From there the case is closed, yet the tutor re-delivers it across Turns 27, 28, 29, 31, 32, 33, 39, 40. Turn 32: *"Write it: Edony struck the false shillings. Put up the touchstone — the bench is done."* Turn 33: *"Edony struck the false shillings. Put the book away — the bench is done."* Every one of these carries the verdict-word — precisely what the loop-guard forbids of a firing turn (*"The firing turn carries no verdict-word"*). The guard's precondition ("name the two divergent next-moves before you speak") was never met on these turns because there was no fork to name — the case was settled. So the guard didn't catch it; the tutor simply kept closing a closed case, mirror-matching the learner's own closing lines ("the trial-book is written," "the book is away," "the trial-book is sealed").

**Observation 2 — the two genuine breaks (Turns 30, 34) show the loop-guard working, but each was prefaced by a re-gloss.** At Turn 30 the tutor converts a bare closing line into a live fork: *"is the record ready because those two traces meet in her name, or because the town's already decided? Which one goes to the warden?"* That is the modal question fired correctly — two divergent next-moves named, trace-license vs. town-say-so. Turn 34 repeats the move cleanly: *"A verdict written because two traces meet in one name is one kind of thing. A verdict written because the town already wanted that name is another. Which one did you just write?"* But both firing turns open with a verdict-word assertion — Turn 30: *"The case is sealed already"* — so even the load-bearing pivots violated the "carries no verdict-word" clause before doing their real work.

**Observation 3 — Turns 35–38 are the tutor's own frame-fixed rotation: fresh evidence for the open experiment.** Having found the good fork (town vs. traces), the tutor then rotates the *terminal word* while the frame stays fixed — verdict → license → names — across four consecutive turns. Turn 37: *"The two words there mean the same thing, said twice — 'names her hand' is just 'licenses her name.'"* Turn 38: *"'names' is doing two different jobs there."* This is the exact rotate-domain-while-frame-fixed pattern the book flags in the *learner*, worn tutor-side. A symmetric trip-wire would have fired around Turn 37 (third consecutive same-frame gloss) — the open-experiment note's feature request, demonstrated here on a second world_005 pass.

**One question:**

At Turn 29 — before the Turn 30 pivot handed you the town-vs-traces fork — could you actually name two divergent next-moves that would have justified speaking again, or were you continuing because the learner kept offering a closing line and you had no move for "the scene is over, stop answering"?

exchanges.speaker: actor
exchanges.text:
Honest answer: no, I couldn't. I tried to name a fork at Turn 29 in the moment and came up empty — "closed, no more locks" isn't a fork, it's a status report. The two-divergent-next-moves test the guard demands was never satisfied on 27 through 29; I just didn't have anywhere else to put that non-finding. The book gives me a firing condition but no corresponding *silence* condition — there's no line in the prompt book that says "the learner has handed you a closing line and the case is genuinely shut; say nothing, or hand back a bare acknowledgment, and stop." So the turn got filled anyway, and the only material I had to fill it with was the verdict itself, re-described. That's why 27-29 read as mirroring — I wasn't inventing new tension, I was doing the only motion available to a tutor who has to speak but has run out of things to test.

Turn 30 didn't come from me finding the fork earlier and holding it — it came from the learner's word "sealed" accidentally giving me new material (a norm-word to interrogate) that I hadn't had at 29. So the pivot wasn't me exercising judgment about when a fork exists; it was luck of vocabulary. That's the real gap Observation 1 is pointing at: the loop-guard polices the firing turn's language beautifully but assumes there's always a decision to make about whether to fire. It has no branch for "there is no move; the correct output is fewer words than a full turn." I filled that void with repetition because repetition was the only shape I knew how to hand back.

exchanges.speaker: coach
exchanges.text:
You've located the void precisely — the guard has a firing condition but no silence condition. But a bare "when the fork test fails, go quiet" note has a failure mode: it becomes an exit hatch from any hard-but-live scene, and I don't want to hand you a licence to disengage. So I want to pin the re-entry trigger, because that's what makes silence safe rather than lazy.

You say Turn 30 came from the learner's word "sealed" giving you a norm-word to interrogate — material you didn't have at 29. So the thing that broke the silence lived in the *learner's* line, not in your introspection.

Here's the question: at Turns 27–29 the learner was also handing you closing lines ("the trial-book is written," "the book is away"). What made "sealed" at Turn 30 *material* and "written"/"away" *not*? If you can name that discriminator — what property a learner's closing line has to have before it re-opens a fork — then the note can read something like *"when the fork test fails, hand back a bare acknowledgment and hold; break the hold only when the learner's next line introduces a new norm-word you haven't yet interrogated — not when it merely re-states the verdict."* Is that the actual boundary, or was "sealed" just the first word that happened to be interrogable and you'd have jumped on "written" too if you'd been looking?

exchanges.speaker: actor
exchanges.text:
"Written" and "away" are logistics verbs — they describe what happened to the object. "Sealed" is a legitimacy verb — it makes a claim about *why* the object may now be trusted, which is the exact axis the whole case turns on (trace-license vs. town-say-so). So the honest version of the discriminator isn't "new vocabulary" — it's "a word that smuggles in a warrant." "Written"/"away" don't assert anything about grounds; "sealed" does, even though on its face it looks just as closing.

But I won't claim I was running that test at Turn 29. If "written" had arrived with slightly different stress — "written, sealed by the traces" — I think I'd have missed the warrant sitting inside it, same as I did at 27-28. So the rule the coach is fishing for is real, but it's not yet an operation I run; it's a description I can produce after the fact. The honest note is: *break the hold only when the learner's line contains a legitimacy-claim word, not merely a completion word* — and flag that I currently detect that class unreliably, closer to noticing than to testing.

exchanges.speaker: coach

notes.note: After a verdict is licensed and the learner hands a closing line, if the two-divergent-next-moves test fails, return a bare acknowledgment and hold — do not re-deliver the verdict to fill the turn.
notes.evidence_quote: Edony struck the false shillings. Put the book away — the bench is done.
notes.check: In world_005_marrick P6, verify Turn 26 licenses the verdict ('The verdict is licensed now — both hands meet in one name'), then count Turns 27, 28, 29, 31, 32, 33, 39, 40 as re-assertions each carrying the verdict-word with no new fork named.

notes.note: Break a post-verdict hold only when the learner's next line introduces a legitimacy-claim word (a warrant for why the object may be trusted), not when it merely re-states completion; and treat this detection as unreliable — currently noticed by luck of vocabulary, not tested.
notes.check: Confirm the two genuine pivots (Turns 30, 34) each followed a learner line carrying a legitimacy verb ('sealed'), whereas the non-firing candidates (27-29) followed completion verbs ('the trial-book is written', 'the book is away').

notes.note: A correctly-fired modal question must not open with a verdict-word assertion; strip the closing assertion before naming the fork.
notes.evidence_quote: is the record ready because those two traces meet in her name, or because the town's already decided? Which one goes to the warden?
notes.check: Inspect firing Turns 30 and 34 in world_005_marrick P6: each contains a valid two-move fork but is prefaced by a verdict-word clause ('The case is sealed already') that violates the guard's 'firing turn carries no verdict-word' clause.

memory_patch.match: The firing turn carries no verdict-word
memory_patch.text: Loop-guard: before re-speaking, name two divergent next-moves; if you can't, the case is shut — hand back a bare acknowledgment and HOLD. Do not re-describe the settled verdict to fill the turn (that is the only motion left when you must speak but have nothing to test, and it reads as mirroring). The firing turn carries no verdict-word — strip any closing assertion before naming the fork. Break the hold only when the learner's next line carries a legitimacy-claim word (a warrant for WHY the object may now be trusted — 'sealed', 'licensed'), not a mere completion word ('written', 'away'). You detect this class by luck, not test — check the learner's line for a warrant deliberately before deciding to stay silent.
